stem: An attacker is trying to guess a user's password.
model_answer: A brute-force attack tries every possible combination of characters in turn (a, b, … aa, ab, … ). It always succeeds eventually but the time grows exponentially with password length. A dictionary attack is faster — it tries entries from a list of common words or leaked passwords first. Dictionary attacks succeed quickly against weak passwords (`password`, `12345678`, `qwerty`); brute force is the fallback when the password isn't in the dictionary. The defence in both cases is the same: long passwords mixing word classes, account-lockout after N failed attempts, and a hash that is slow to compute (Argon2 / bcrypt).
review_notes: Teacher-marked. Award marks for: brute-force exhaustive search, dictionary tries common words first, one shared mitigation (length / lockout / slow hash).

parts.label: (a)
parts.prompt: Compare a brute-force attack and a dictionary attack, and describe one mitigation that defends against both.

mark_points.text: brute force = tries every combination of characters in turn
dictionary = tries common words / leaked passwords first (faster against weak passwords)
shared mitigation: long/complex passwords, account lockout, slow password hash
